quizzes.q: 'Transports blood throughout the body. Components: heart (pumps blood), arteries ...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Circulatory System' is defined as: Transports blood throughout the body. Components: heart (pumps blood), arteries (carry blood away from heart), veins (carry blood to heart), capillaries (exchange gases/nutrients). Blood carries oxygen (via hemoglobin in red blood cells), nutrients, hormones, and waste products. Double circulation: pulmonary circuit (heart → lungs → heart) and systemic circuit (heart → body → heart).

quizzes.q: 'Brings oxygen into the body and removes carbon dioxide. Path: nose/mouth → phary...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Respiratory System' is defined as: Brings oxygen into the body and removes carbon dioxide. Path: nose/mouth → pharynx → larynx → trachea → bronchi → bronchioles → alveoli (gas exchange). Gas exchange occurs in alveoli: O₂ diffuses into blood, CO₂ diffuses out. Breathing is controlled by the diaphragm and intercostal muscles.

quizzes.q: 'Breaks down food into nutrients the body can absorb. Path: mouth (mechanical + c...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Digestive System' is defined as: Breaks down food into nutrients the body can absorb. Path: mouth (mechanical + chemical digestion) → esophagus → stomach (acid, enzymes) → small intestine (nutrient absorption, majority of digestion) → large intestine (water absorption) → rectum. Key organs: liver (bile production), pancreas (enzymes, insulin), gallbladder (stores bile).

quizzes.q: 'Controls and coordinates body functions. Central Nervous System (CNS) = brain + ...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Nervous System' is defined as: Controls and coordinates body functions. Central Nervous System (CNS) = brain + spinal cord. Peripheral Nervous System (PNS) = all other nerves. Neurons transmit electrical signals. Reflexes are rapid, automatic responses (reflex arc: receptor → sensory neuron → interneuron → motor neuron → effector).

quizzes.q: 'Produces hormones that regulate body functions. Glands: pituitary (master gland)...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Endocrine System' is defined as: Produces hormones that regulate body functions. Glands: pituitary (master gland), thyroid (metabolism), adrenal (stress response), pancreas (insulin/glucagon for blood sugar), ovaries/testes (sex hormones). Hormones travel through blood to target organs. Slower but longer-lasting than nervous system.

quizzes.q: 'Removes waste products from the body. Kidneys filter blood to produce urine (ure...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Excretory System' is defined as: Removes waste products from the body. Kidneys filter blood to produce urine (urea, excess water, salts). Ureters carry urine to bladder. Skin excretes sweat (water + salts). Lungs excrete CO₂. Liver converts toxic ammonia to urea.

quizzes.q: 'The maintenance of a stable internal environment in the body. Includes regulatio...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Homeostasis' is defined as: The maintenance of a stable internal environment in the body. Includes regulation of temperature (thermoregulation), blood sugar (glucose regulation), blood pH (acid-base balance), water balance (osmoregulation), and blood pressure.

quizzes.q: 'The most common feedback mechanism. The body detects a change from the set point...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Negative Feedback' is defined as: The most common feedback mechanism. The body detects a change from the set point and activates mechanisms to reverse it. Example: Body temperature rises → hypothalamus detects → blood vessels dilate, sweat produced → temperature drops → returns to 37°C. Example: Blood sugar rises → pancreas releases insulin → cells absorb glucose → blood sugar drops → insulin release stops.

quizzes.q: 'Amplifies a change (pushes further from set point). Rare in the body. Example: C...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Positive Feedback' is defined as: Amplifies a change (pushes further from set point). Rare in the body. Example: Childbirth — uterine contractions stimulate more oxytocin → stronger contractions → more oxytocin → until birth. Example: Blood clotting — platelets release chemicals that attract more platelets → clot forms.

quizzes.q: '1. Organism = individual living thing. 2. Population = all individuals of one sp...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Levels of Ecology' is defined as: 1. Organism = individual living thing. 2. Population = all individuals of one species in an area. 3. Community = all populations of different species in an area. 4. Ecosystem = community + abiotic factors (soil, water, climate). 5. Biome = large area with similar climate and vegetation (tropical rainforest, desert, tundra). 6. Biosphere = all ecosystems on Earth combined.

quizzes.q: 'Food chain: linear path of energy flow (producer → primary consumer → secondary ...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Food Chains and Food Webs' is defined as: Food chain: linear path of energy flow (producer → primary consumer → secondary consumer → tertiary consumer). Food web: interconnected food chains in an ecosystem. Producers (autotrophs) make their own food (plants, algae). Consumers (heterotrophs) eat other organisms. Decomposers break down dead matter (bacteria, fungi).

quizzes.q: 'Unlike energy, nutrients cycle through ecosystems. Carbon cycle: CO₂ in atmosphe...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Nutrient Cycles' is defined as: Unlike energy, nutrients cycle through ecosystems. Carbon cycle: CO₂ in atmosphere → photosynthesis → organic molecules → respiration/decomposition → CO₂. Nitrogen cycle: N₂ in atmosphere → nitrogen fixation (bacteria) → nitrates in soil → plants → animals → decomposition → denitrification → N₂. Water cycle: evaporation → condensation → precipitation → runoff/infiltration → evaporation.

quizzes.q: 'The variety of life in an ecosystem. Includes: 1. Species diversity (number of d...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Biodiversity' is defined as: The variety of life in an ecosystem. Includes: 1. Species diversity (number of different species). 2. Genetic diversity (variation within species). 3. Ecosystem diversity (variety of ecosystems). High biodiversity = healthy, resilient ecosystem. Low biodiversity = vulnerable to disturbances.

quizzes.q: 'Key plant structures: Roots (absorb water and minerals), stems (support and tran...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Plant Biology (Botany)' is defined as: Key plant structures: Roots (absorb water and minerals), stems (support and transport), leaves (photosynthesis), flowers (reproduction). Plant tissues: Dermal (outer covering), vascular (xylem = water up, phloem = sugars down), ground (photosynthesis, storage). Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂.

quizzes.q: 'Animals are classified by: Body symmetry (bilateral = left/right, radial = circu...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Animal Biology (Zoology)' is defined as: Animals are classified by: Body symmetry (bilateral = left/right, radial = circular). Body cavity (coelom = true body cavity). Germ layers (ectoderm, mesoderm, endoderm). Development (protostome = mouth first, deuterostome = anus first). Major phyla: Porifera (sponges), Cnidaria (jellyfish), Arthropoda (insects, spiders), Mollusca (snails, clams), Chordata (vertebrates).

Food web: interconnected food chains in an ecosystem. Producers (autotrophs) make their own food (plants, algae). Consumers (heterotrophs) eat other organisms. Decomposers break down dead matter (bacteria, fungi).

quizzes.q: Complete: ___ is defined as 'Unlike energy, nutrients cycle through ecosystems. Carbon cycle: CO₂ in atmosphe...'
quizzes.explanation: 'Nutrient Cycles' is defined as: Unlike energy, nutrients cycle through ecosystems. Carbon cycle: CO₂ in atmosphere → photosynthesis → organic molecules → respiration/decomposition → CO₂. Nitrogen cycle: N₂ in atmosphere → nitrogen fixation (bacteria) → nitrates in soil → plants → animals → decomposition → denitrification → N₂. Water cycle: evaporation → condensation → precipitation → runoff/infiltration → evaporation.

quizzes.q: Complete: ___ is defined as 'The variety of life in an ecosystem. Includes: 1. Species diversity (number of d...'
quizzes.explanation: 'Biodiversity' is defined as: The variety of life in an ecosystem. Includes: 1. Species diversity (number of different species). 2. Genetic diversity (variation within species). 3. Ecosystem diversity (variety of ecosystems). High biodiversity = healthy, resilient ecosystem. Low biodiversity = vulnerable to disturbances.

quizzes.q: Complete: ___ is defined as 'Key plant structures: Roots (absorb water and minerals), stems (support and tran...'
quizzes.explanation: 'Plant Biology (Botany)' is defined as: Key plant structures: Roots (absorb water and minerals), stems (support and transport), leaves (photosynthesis), flowers (reproduction). Plant tissues: Dermal (outer covering), vascular (xylem = water up, phloem = sugars down), ground (photosynthesis, storage). Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂.

quizzes.q: Complete: ___ is defined as 'Animals are classified by: Body symmetry (bilateral = left/right, radial = circu...'
quizzes.explanation: 'Animal Biology (Zoology)' is defined as: Animals are classified by: Body symmetry (bilateral = left/right, radial = circular). Body cavity (coelom = true body cavity). Germ layers (ectoderm, mesoderm, endoderm). Development (protostome = mouth first, deuterostome = anus first). Major phyla: Porifera (sponges), Cnidaria (jellyfish), Arthropoda (insects, spiders), Mollusca (snails, clams), Chordata (vertebrates).

quizzes.q: A student encounters 'Transports blood throughout the body. Components: ...' — which concept does this illustrate?
quizzes.explanation: This describes Circulatory System: Transports blood throughout the body. Components: heart (pumps blood), arteries (carry blood away from heart), veins (carry blood to heart), capillaries (exchange gases/nutrients). Blood carries oxygen (via hemoglobin in red blood cells), nutrients, hormones, and waste products. Double circulation: pulmonary circuit (heart → lungs → heart) and systemic circuit (heart → body → heart).

quizzes.q: A student encounters 'Brings oxygen into the body and removes carbon dio...' — which concept does this illustrate?
quizzes.explanation: This describes Respiratory System: Brings oxygen into the body and removes carbon dioxide. Path: nose/mouth → pharynx → larynx → trachea → bronchi → bronchioles → alveoli (gas exchange). Gas exchange occurs in alveoli: O₂ diffuses into blood, CO₂ diffuses out. Breathing is controlled by the diaphragm and intercostal muscles.

quizzes.q: A student encounters 'Breaks down food into nutrients the body can absor...' — which concept does this illustrate?
quizzes.explanation: This describes Digestive System: Breaks down food into nutrients the body can absorb. Path: mouth (mechanical + chemical digestion) → esophagus → stomach (acid, enzymes) → small intestine (nutrient absorption, majority of digestion) → large intestine (water absorption) → rectum. Key organs: liver (bile production), pancreas (enzymes, insulin), gallbladder (stores bile).

quizzes.q: A student encounters 'Controls and coordinates body functions. Central N...' — which concept does this illustrate?
quizzes.explanation: This describes Nervous System: Controls and coordinates body functions. Central Nervous System (CNS) = brain + spinal cord. Peripheral Nervous System (PNS) = all other nerves. Neurons transmit electrical signals. Reflexes are rapid, automatic responses (reflex arc: receptor → sensory neuron → interneuron → motor neuron → effector).

quizzes.q: A student encounters 'Produces hormones that regulate body functions. Gl...' — which concept does this illustrate?
quizzes.explanation: This describes Endocrine System: Produces hormones that regulate body functions. Glands: pituitary (master gland), thyroid (metabolism), adrenal (stress response), pancreas (insulin/glucagon for blood sugar), ovaries/testes (sex hormones). Hormones travel through blood to target organs. Slower but longer-lasting than nervous system.

quizzes.q: A student encounters 'Removes waste products from the body. Kidneys filt...' — which concept does this illustrate?
quizzes.explanation: This describes Excretory System: Removes waste products from the body. Kidneys filter blood to produce urine (urea, excess water, salts). Ureters carry urine to bladder. Skin excretes sweat (water + salts). Lungs excrete CO₂. Liver converts toxic ammonia to urea.

quizzes.q: A student encounters 'The maintenance of a stable internal environment i...' — which concept does this illustrate?
quizzes.explanation: This describes Homeostasis: The maintenance of a stable internal environment in the body. Includes regulation of temperature (thermoregulation), blood sugar (glucose regulation), blood pH (acid-base balance), water balance (osmoregulation), and blood pressure.

quizzes.q: A student encounters 'The most common feedback mechanism. The body detec...' — which concept does this illustrate?
quizzes.explanation: This describes Negative Feedback: The most common feedback mechanism. The body detects a change from the set point and activates mechanisms to reverse it. Example: Body temperature rises → hypothalamus detects → blood vessels dilate, sweat produced → temperature drops → returns to 37°C. Example: Blood sugar rises → pancreas releases insulin → cells absorb glucose → blood sugar drops → insulin release stops.

quizzes.q: A student encounters 'Amplifies a change (pushes further from set point)...' — which concept does this illustrate?
quizzes.explanation: This describes Positive Feedback: Amplifies a change (pushes further from set point). Rare in the body. Example: Childbirth — uterine contractions stimulate more oxytocin → stronger contractions → more oxytocin → until birth. Example: Blood clotting — platelets release chemicals that attract more platelets → clot forms.

quizzes.q: A student encounters '1. Organism = individual living thing. 2. Populati...' — which concept does this illustrate?
quizzes.explanation: This describes Levels of Ecology: 1. Organism = individual living thing. 2. Population = all individuals of one species in an area. 3. Community = all populations of different species in an area. 4. Ecosystem = community + abiotic factors (soil, water, climate). 5. Biome = large area with similar climate and vegetation (tropical rainforest, desert, tundra). 6. Biosphere = all ecosystems on Earth combined.

quizzes.q: How many valence electrons do halogens have?
quizzes.explanation: Halogens (Group 17: F, Cl, Br, I, At) have 7 valence electrons. They need just one more electron to complete their outer shell, making them highly reactive nonmetals.

quizzes.q: Which gas law is P₁V₁ = P₂V₂?
quizzes.explanation: Boyle's Law states that at constant temperature, pressure and volume are inversely proportional: P₁V₁ = P₂V₂. As pressure increases, volume decreases.

quizzes.q: What is the atomic number of Carbon?
quizzes.explanation: Carbon has atomic number 6, meaning it has 6 protons in its nucleus. Its electron configuration is 2,4 — giving it 4 valence electrons and the ability to form 4 bonds.

quizzes.q: Which is the most electronegative element?
quizzes.explanation: Fluorine is the most electronegative element on the Pauling scale (3.98). It has a strong tendency to attract electrons in chemical bonds due to its small atomic size and high nuclear charge.

quizzes.q: What type of bond is NaCl?
quizzes.explanation: NaCl (sodium chloride) is an ionic compound formed by the transfer of electrons from sodium (metal) to chlorine (nonmetal), creating Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held together by electrostatic attraction.

quizzes.q: How many grams does it have?
quizzes.explanation: This question appears to refer to the molar mass of carbon-12. One mole of carbon-12 atoms has a mass of exactly 12 grams by definition of the mole.

quizzes.q: What is the charge of a neutron?
quizzes.explanation: Neutrons have no electric charge (charge = 0). They are found in the nucleus alongside protons. Protons have +1 charge and electrons have -1 charge.

quizzes.q: The mass number of an atom equals:
quizzes.explanation: Mass number (A) = number of protons + number of neutrons in the nucleus. Electrons have negligible mass and are not included in the mass number.

quizzes.q: What is the maximum number of electrons in the second shell?
quizzes.explanation: The correct answer is "6."

quizzes.q: Which subshell is filled first according to the Aufbau principle?
quizzes.explanation: The correct answer is "2p."

quizzes.q: The shape of an s orbital is:
quizzes.explanation: The correct answer is "Dumbbell."

quizzes.q: What is the electron configuration of Oxygen (atomic number 8)?
quizzes.explanation: The correct answer is "1s² 2s² 2p²."

quizzes.q: Which of the following has the largest atomic radius?
quizzes.explanation: The correct answer is "Na."

quizzes.q: What is the molecular geometry of CH₄ (methane)?
quizzes.explanation: The correct answer is "Trigonal planar."

quizzes.q: According to Hund's rule, how are electrons filled in degenerate orbitals?
opts: Paired in the first orbital first
One in each orbital before pairing
quizzes.explanation: The correct answer is "Paired in the first orbital first."

quizzes.q: Which type of hybridization does carbon exhibit in ethene (C₂H₄)?
quizzes.explanation: The correct answer is "sp."

quizzes.q: 1 mole of gas at STP occupies:
quizzes.explanation: At Standard Temperature and Pressure (STP: 0°C, 1 atm), one mole of any ideal gas occupies 22.4 liters. This is known as the molar volume of a gas.

quizzes.q: Which is a strong acid?
quizzes.explanation: HCl (hydrochloric acid) is a strong acid — it completely dissociates in water. Acetic acid, citric acid, and carbonic acid are weak acids that only partially dissociate.

quizzes.q: What is the pH of pure water?
quizzes.explanation: Pure water at 25°C has a pH of 7, which is neutral (neither acidic nor basic). pH < 7 is acidic and pH > 7 is basic.

quizzes.q: In an exothermic reaction, energy is:
quizzes.explanation: In an exothermic reaction, energy is released to the surroundings (usually as heat), making the surroundings warmer. The products have less energy than the reactants.

quizzes.q: What is the balanced equation for combustion of methane?
quizzes.explanation: The balanced equation for methane combustion is CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O. One molecule of methane reacts with two molecules of oxygen to produce one CO₂ and two water molecules.

quizzes.q: What is an acid with pH less than?
quizzes.explanation: A solution is acidic when its pH is less than 7. The lower the pH, the stronger the acid. pH 7 is neutral, and above 7 is basic/alkaline.

quizzes.q: In the reaction 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O, how many oxygen atoms are on the left?
quizzes.explanation: In 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O, the left side has O₂ which contains 2 oxygen atoms. Counting atoms on both sides is essential for balancing equations.

quizzes.q: What is oxidation?
quizzes.explanation: Oxidation is the loss of electrons by an atom, ion, or molecule. It always occurs together with reduction (gain of electrons) in a redox reaction.

quizzes.q: What is the mole ratio in 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O?
quizzes.explanation: The mole ratio from the balanced equation 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O is 2:1:2 — meaning 2 moles of hydrogen react with 1 mole of oxygen to produce 2 moles of water.

quizzes.q: How many grams are in 1 mole of H₂O?
quizzes.explanation: The molar mass of H₂O = (2 × 1) + 16 = 18 g/mol. Hydrogen has atomic mass ~1 and oxygen has atomic mass ~16.

quizzes.q: Avogadro's number is:
quizzes.explanation: Avogadro's number (6.02 × 10²³) is the number of particles (atoms, molecules, ions) in one mole of a substance. It bridges the atomic and macroscopic scales.

quizzes.q: The molar mass of CO₂ is:
quizzes.explanation: Molar mass of CO₂ = 12 + (2 × 16) = 44 g/mol. Carbon is 12 g/mol and each oxygen is 16 g/mol.

quizzes.q: What is the limiting reagent?
opts: The reagent that runs out first
quizzes.explanation: The limiting reagent is the reactant that is completely consumed first in a chemical reaction, limiting the amount of product that can be formed.

quizzes.q: How many moles are in 44g of CO₂?
quizzes.explanation: Moles = mass / molar mass = 44g / 44 g/mol = 1 mol. The molar mass of CO₂ is 44 g/mol (12 + 2×16).

quizzes.q: The theoretical yield is:
quizzes.explanation: Theoretical yield is the maximum amount of product that can be formed from given amounts of reactants, calculated using stoichiometry assuming 100% reaction efficiency.

quizzes.q: Percent yield equals:
quizzes.explanation: Percent yield = (actual yield / theoretical yield) × 100. It measures the efficiency of a reaction — how much product was actually obtained compared to the maximum possible.

quizzes.q: What is the molar volume of a gas at STP?
quizzes.explanation: The molar volume of any ideal gas at STP (0°C, 1 atm) is 22.4 L/mol. This is derived from the ideal gas law at standard conditions.

quizzes.q: The empirical formula shows:
quizzes.explanation: The empirical formula shows the simplest whole-number ratio of elements in a compound (e.g., CH₂O for glucose). The molecular formula shows the actual number of atoms (e.g., C₆H₁₂O₆).

quizzes.q: How many molecules are in 2 moles of H₂O?
quizzes.explanation: Number of molecules = moles × Avogadro's number = 2 × 6.02 × 10²³ = 1.204 × 10²⁴ molecules.

quizzes.q: 'A process in which substances (reactants) are converted into new substances (pro...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Chemical Reaction' is defined as: A process in which substances (reactants) are converted into new substances (products) through the breaking and forming of chemical bonds.

quizzes.q: 'Adding coefficients to chemical formulas so that the number of atoms of each ele...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Balancing an Equation' is defined as: Adding coefficients to chemical formulas so that the number of atoms of each element is the same on both sides of the equation.

quizzes.q: 'The SI unit for amount of substance. One mole contains exactly 6.022 × 10²³ part...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Mole (mol)' is defined as: The SI unit for amount of substance. One mole contains exactly 6.022 × 10²³ particles (Avogadro's number). This number is so large because atoms are incredibly tiny.

quizzes.q: 'Nₐ = 6.022 × 10²³ particles/mol. This is the number of atoms, molecules, ions, o...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Avogadro's Number' is defined as: Nₐ = 6.022 × 10²³ particles/mol. This is the number of atoms, molecules, ions, or formula units in one mole of any substance.

quizzes.q: 'The mass of one mole of a substance, expressed in grams per mole (g/mol). Numeri...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Molar Mass' is defined as: The mass of one mole of a substance, expressed in grams per mole (g/mol). Numerically equal to the atomic/molecular weight from the periodic table.

quizzes.q: 'Force per unit area exerted by gas molecules colliding with container walls. Mea...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Pressure' is defined as: Force per unit area exerted by gas molecules colliding with container walls. Measured in atmospheres (atm), mmHg, or kilopascals (kPa). Standard pressure = 1 atm = 760 mmHg = 101.3 kPa.

quizzes.q: '0°C (273.15 K) and 1 atm. At STP, 1 mole of any ideal gas occupies exactly 22.4 ...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'STP (Standard Temperature and Pressure)' is defined as: 0°C (273.15 K) and 1 atm. At STP, 1 mole of any ideal gas occupies exactly 22.4 L.

quizzes.q: 'A homogeneous mixture where a solute is uniformly distributed in a solvent. Exam...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Solution' is defined as: A homogeneous mixture where a solute is uniformly distributed in a solvent. Examples: saltwater, sugar dissolved in tea, air (gas mixture).

quizzes.q: 'The most common unit of concentration. Molarity = moles of solute ÷ liters of so...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Molarity (M)' is defined as: The most common unit of concentration. Molarity = moles of solute ÷ liters of solution. Expressed as mol/L or M. Example: 2 mol NaCl in 1 L solution = 2 M NaCl.

quizzes.q: 'The process of adding more solvent to a solution, which decreases its concentrat...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Dilution' is defined as: The process of adding more solvent to a solution, which decreases its concentration but does not change the number of moles of solute.

quizzes.q: 'The number of particles a solute produces when dissolved. NaCl → Na⁺ + Cl⁻, so i...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'van't Hoff Factor (i)' is defined as: The number of particles a solute produces when dissolved. NaCl → Na⁺ + Cl⁻, so i = 2. CaCl₂ → Ca²⁺ + 2Cl⁻, so i = 3. Glucose (non-electrolyte) does not dissociate, so i = 1.

quizzes.q: 'A substance that produces hydrogen ions (H⁺) when dissolved in water. Example: H...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Arrhenius Acid' is defined as: A substance that produces hydrogen ions (H⁺) when dissolved in water. Example: HCl → H⁺ + Cl⁻.

quizzes.q: 'A substance that produces hydroxide ions (OH⁻) when dissolved in water. Example:...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Arrhenius Base' is defined as: A substance that produces hydroxide ions (OH⁻) when dissolved in water. Example: NaOH → Na⁺ + OH⁻.

quizzes.q: 'A proton (H⁺) donor. This definition is broader than Arrhenius because it applie...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Brønsted-Lowry Acid' is defined as: A proton (H⁺) donor. This definition is broader than Arrhenius because it applies even without water.

quizzes.q: 'A proton (H⁺) acceptor. Example: NH₃ + H₂O → NH₄⁺ + OH⁻. Here NH₃ accepts a prot...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Brønsted-Lowry Base' is defined as: A proton (H⁺) acceptor. Example: NH₃ + H₂O → NH₄⁺ + OH⁻. Here NH₃ accepts a proton from water.

quizzes.q: 'An acid-base reaction that produces a salt and water. General form: Acid + Base ...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Neutralization Reaction' is defined as: An acid-base reaction that produces a salt and water. General form: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

quizzes.q: Which term matches this description: 'A process in which substances (reactants) are converted into new substances (pro...'?
quizzes.explanation: 'Chemical Reaction' is defined as: A process in which substances (reactants) are converted into new substances (products) through the breaking and forming of chemical bonds.

quizzes.q: Which term matches this description: 'Adding coefficients to chemical formulas so that the number of atoms of each ele...'?
quizzes.explanation: 'Balancing an Equation' is defined as: Adding coefficients to chemical formulas so that the number of atoms of each element is the same on both sides of the equation.

quizzes.q: Which term matches this description: 'The SI unit for amount of substance. One mole contains exactly 6.022 × 10²³ part...'?
quizzes.explanation: 'Mole (mol)' is defined as: The SI unit for amount of substance. One mole contains exactly 6.022 × 10²³ particles (Avogadro's number). This number is so large because atoms are incredibly tiny.

quizzes.q: Which term matches this description: 'The mass of one mole of a substance, expressed in grams per mole (g/mol). Numeri...'?
quizzes.explanation: 'Molar Mass' is defined as: The mass of one mole of a substance, expressed in grams per mole (g/mol). Numerically equal to the atomic/molecular weight from the periodic table.

quizzes.q: Which term matches this description: 'Force per unit area exerted by gas molecules colliding with container walls. Mea...'?
quizzes.explanation: 'Pressure' is defined as: Force per unit area exerted by gas molecules colliding with container walls. Measured in atmospheres (atm), mmHg, or kilopascals (kPa). Standard pressure = 1 atm = 760 mmHg = 101.3 kPa.

quizzes.q: Which term matches this description: '0°C (273.15 K) and 1 atm. At STP, 1 mole of any ideal gas occupies exactly 22.4 ...'?
quizzes.explanation: 'STP (Standard Temperature and Pressure)' is defined as: 0°C (273.15 K) and 1 atm. At STP, 1 mole of any ideal gas occupies exactly 22.4 L.

quizzes.q: Which term matches this description: 'A homogeneous mixture where a solute is uniformly distributed in a solvent. Exam...'?
quizzes.explanation: 'Solution' is defined as: A homogeneous mixture where a solute is uniformly distributed in a solvent. Examples: saltwater, sugar dissolved in tea, air (gas mixture).

quizzes.q: Which term matches this description: 'The most common unit of concentration. Molarity = moles of solute ÷ liters of so...'?
quizzes.explanation: 'Molarity (M)' is defined as: The most common unit of concentration. Molarity = moles of solute ÷ liters of solution. Expressed as mol/L or M. Example: 2 mol NaCl in 1 L solution = 2 M NaCl.

quizzes.q: Which term matches this description: 'The process of adding more solvent to a solution, which decreases its concentrat...'?
quizzes.explanation: 'Dilution' is defined as: The process of adding more solvent to a solution, which decreases its concentration but does not change the number of moles of solute.

quizzes.q: Which term matches this description: 'The number of particles a solute produces when dissolved. NaCl → Na⁺ + Cl⁻, so i...'?
quizzes.explanation: 'van't Hoff Factor (i)' is defined as: The number of particles a solute produces when dissolved. NaCl → Na⁺ + Cl⁻, so i = 2. CaCl₂ → Ca²⁺ + 2Cl⁻, so i = 3. Glucose (non-electrolyte) does not dissociate, so i = 1.

quizzes.q: Which term matches this description: 'A substance that produces hydrogen ions (H⁺) when dissolved in water. Example: H...'?
quizzes.explanation: 'Arrhenius Acid' is defined as: A substance that produces hydrogen ions (H⁺) when dissolved in water. Example: HCl → H⁺ + Cl⁻.

quizzes.q: Which term matches this description: 'A substance that produces hydroxide ions (OH⁻) when dissolved in water. Example:...'?
quizzes.explanation: 'Arrhenius Base' is defined as: A substance that produces hydroxide ions (OH⁻) when dissolved in water. Example: NaOH → Na⁺ + OH⁻.

quizzes.q: Which term matches this description: 'A proton (H⁺) acceptor. Example: NH₃ + H₂O → NH₄⁺ + OH⁻. Here NH₃ accepts a prot...'?
quizzes.explanation: 'Brønsted-Lowry Base' is defined as: A proton (H⁺) acceptor. Example: NH₃ + H₂O → NH₄⁺ + OH⁻. Here NH₃ accepts a proton from water.

quizzes.q: Which term matches this description: 'An acid-base reaction that produces a salt and water. General form: Acid + Base ...'?
quizzes.explanation: 'Neutralization Reaction' is defined as: An acid-base reaction that produces a salt and water. General form: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

quizzes.q: F = ma is which law?
quizzes.explanation: F = ma is Newton's Second Law of Motion. It states that the force acting on an object equals its mass multiplied by its acceleration.

quizzes.q: What is the formula for kinetic energy?
quizzes.explanation: Kinetic energy is the energy of motion, given by KE = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. This shows that doubling velocity quadruples kinetic energy.

quizzes.q: What is the speed of light in vacuum?
quizzes.explanation: The speed of light in a vacuum is approximately 3 × 10⁸ meters per second (about 300,000 km/s). This is a fundamental constant denoted by 'c'.

quizzes.q: What is the SI unit of force?
quizzes.explanation: The SI unit of force is the Newton (N), named after Sir Isaac Newton. One Newton is the force needed to accelerate 1 kg at 1 m/s².

quizzes.q: An object at rest stays at rest unless acted upon by a force. This is:
quizzes.explanation: Newton's First Law (Law of Inertia) states that an object at rest stays at rest and an object in motion stays in motion at constant velocity, unless acted upon by a net external force.

quizzes.q: Work is calculated as:
quizzes.explanation: Work is calculated as W = F × d, where F is force applied in the direction of displacement and d is the displacement. Work is measured in Joules.

quizzes.q: Momentum equals:
quizzes.explanation: Momentum (p) is the product of mass and velocity: p = mv. It is a vector quantity, meaning it has both magnitude and direction. Momentum is conserved in isolated systems.

quizzes.q: Newton's First Law is also known as:
quizzes.explanation: Newton's First Law is also called the Law of Inertia because it describes how objects resist changes in their state of motion.

quizzes.q: F = ma is:
quizzes.explanation: F = ma is Newton's Second Law, which quantifies the relationship between force, mass, and acceleration. Greater force or less mass means greater acceleration.

quizzes.q: The unit of energy is:
quizzes.explanation: The Joule (J) is the SI unit of energy and work. One Joule equals the work done by a force of one Newton moving an object one meter.

quizzes.q: What is the acceleration due to gravity?
quizzes.explanation: The acceleration due to gravity near Earth's surface is approximately 9.8 m/s² (often rounded to 10 m/s²). This means objects in free fall speed up by 9.8 m/s each second.

quizzes.q: Kinetic energy equals:
quizzes.explanation: Kinetic energy is KE = ½mv². The formula mgh gives gravitational potential energy, mv gives momentum, and Fd gives work.

quizzes.q: Power is measured in:
quizzes.explanation: Power is measured in Watts (W), named after James Watt. One Watt = one Joule/second. Joules measure energy, Newtons measure force, Pascals measure pressure.

quizzes.q: When you push a wall and it doesn't move, you do:
quizzes.explanation: Work requires displacement. If you push a wall and it doesn't move (d = 0), then W = F × 0 = 0. Despite exerting force, no work is done in the physics sense.

quizzes.q: The formula for momentum is:
quizzes.explanation: Momentum formula is p = mv. F = ma is Newton's Second Law, E = mc² is Einstein's mass-energy equivalence, and W = Fd is the work formula.

quizzes.q: An object in free fall has acceleration of:
quizzes.explanation: In free fall, the only force acting is gravity, giving an acceleration of 9.8 m/s² downward (neglecting air resistance). This is true regardless of the object's mass.

quizzes.q: Friction always acts:
quizzes.explanation: Friction is a contact force that always opposes the direction of motion (or intended motion) between two surfaces in contact.

quizzes.q: The work-energy theorem states:
opts: Net work equals change in KE
quizzes.explanation: The work-energy theorem states that the net work done on an object equals its change in kinetic energy: W_net = ΔKE = ½mv₂² - ½mv₁².

quizzes.q: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
quizzes.explanation: The Ohm (Ω) is the SI unit of electrical resistance. Named after Georg Ohm, it measures how much a material opposes the flow of electric current.

quizzes.q: Sound cannot travel through:
quizzes.explanation: Sound is a mechanical wave that requires a medium (solid, liquid, or gas) to travel. It cannot propagate through a vacuum because there are no particles to vibrate.

quizzes.q: Which color of light has the longest wavelength?
quizzes.explanation: In the visible spectrum, red light has the longest wavelength (~700 nm) and lowest frequency, while violet light has the shortest wavelength (~400 nm) and highest frequency.

quizzes.q: Ohm's Law states V = ?
quizzes.explanation: Ohm's Law states V = IR, where V is voltage (Volts), I is current (Amperes), and R is resistance (Ohms). It describes the relationship between these three electrical quantities.

quizzes.q: Total resistance in a series circuit is:
quizzes.explanation: In a series circuit, resistances add up: R_total = R₁ + R₂ + R₃ + ... The current is the same through all components, but voltage is divided.

quizzes.q: Which type of wave needs a medium to travel?
quizzes.explanation: Sound waves are mechanical/longitudinal waves that require a medium (air, water, solids) to propagate. Light, radio, and X-rays are electromagnetic waves that can travel through a vacuum.

quizzes.q: What type of wave is a sound wave?
quizzes.explanation: The correct answer is "Transverse wave."

quizzes.q: The bending of light as it passes from one medium to another is called:
quizzes.explanation: The correct answer is "Diffraction."

quizzes.q: Which part of the electromagnetic spectrum has the highest frequency?
quizzes.explanation: The correct answer is "Ultraviolet rays."

quizzes.q: A converging lens is also known as a:
quizzes.explanation: The correct answer is "Concave lens."

quizzes.q: The SI unit of electric current is:
quizzes.explanation: The correct answer is "Watt."

quizzes.q: When light reflects off a smooth surface, the angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection. This is the law of:
quizzes.explanation: The correct answer is "Refraction."

quizzes.q: In a parallel circuit, the voltage across each branch is:
quizzes.explanation: The correct answer is "Zero."

quizzes.q: Which of the following has the shortest wavelength in the visible spectrum?
quizzes.explanation: The correct answer is "Green."

quizzes.q: Power in an electrical circuit is calculated as:
quizzes.explanation: The correct answer is "P = I/R."

quizzes.q: A concave mirror can produce a real, inverted image when the object is placed:
opts: Between the mirror and its focal point
Between the focal point and the center of curvature
quizzes.explanation: The correct answer is "Between the mirror and its focal point."

quizzes.q: Three resistors of 3Ω, 6Ω, and 6Ω are connected in parallel. What is the total resistance?
quizzes.explanation: The correct answer is "15Ω."

quizzes.q: Which planet is known as the Red Planet?
quizzes.explanation: Mars is called the Red Planet because iron oxide (rust) on its surface gives it a reddish appearance. Venus is the hottest, Jupiter is the largest, and Saturn has rings.

quizzes.q: Which type of rock is formed from cooled magma?
quizzes.explanation: Igneous rocks form when magma (molten rock beneath the surface) or lava (molten rock on the surface) cools and solidifies. Examples include granite and basalt.

quizzes.q: Earth's magnetic field is generated by the:
quizzes.explanation: Earth's magnetic field is generated by convection currents of liquid iron and nickel in the outer core. This dynamo effect creates and sustains the geomagnetic field.

quizzes.q: Which layer of Earth's atmosphere contains the ozone layer?
quizzes.explanation: The ozone layer is located in the stratosphere (about 15-35 km above Earth). It absorbs most of the sun's harmful ultraviolet radiation, protecting life on Earth.

quizzes.q: Photosynthesis occurs in which organelle?
quizzes.explanation: Chloroplasts are the organelles where photosynthesis occurs in plant cells. They contain chlorophyll, the green pigment that captures light energy to convert CO₂ and H₂O into glucose.

quizzes.q: What causes tides on Earth?
quizzes.explanation: Tides are primarily caused by the gravitational pull of the Moon on Earth's oceans. The Sun also contributes, but the Moon's proximity makes it the dominant factor.

quizzes.q: Which layer of the atmosphere do we live in?
quizzes.explanation: The troposphere is the lowest layer of Earth's atmosphere (0-12 km), where we live and where weather occurs. Temperature generally decreases with altitude in this layer.

quizzes.q: What is a hypothesis?
quizzes.explanation: A hypothesis is a proposed explanation for a phenomenon that can be tested through experimentation. It is not yet proven — it is a testable prediction.

quizzes.q: In a controlled experiment, the control group:
quizzes.explanation: In a controlled experiment, the control group does not receive the experimental treatment and serves as a baseline for comparison with the experimental group.

quizzes.q: What is the unit of force in SI?
quizzes.explanation: The SI unit of force is the Newton (N). Joules measure energy, Watts measure power, and Pascals measure pressure.

quizzes.q: Photosynthesis converts:
quizzes.explanation: Photosynthesis converts carbon dioxide (CO₂) and water (H₂O) into glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆) and oxygen (O₂) using light energy: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂.

quizzes.q: DNA stands for:
quizzes.explanation: DNA stands for Deoxyribonucleic Acid — the molecule that carries genetic information in all living organisms and many viruses.

quizzes.q: The pH of pure water is:
quizzes.explanation: Pure water at 25°C has a pH of 7, which is neutral. It has equal concentrations of H⁺ and OH⁻ ions (both 10⁻⁷ M).

quizzes.q: What is the speed of light?
quizzes.explanation: The speed of light in a vacuum is approximately 3 × 10⁸ m/s (about 300,000 km/s). This is the universal speed limit — nothing travels faster.

quizzes.q: An atom consists of:
quizzes.explanation: An atom consists of three subatomic particles: protons (+) and neutrons (0) in the nucleus, and electrons (-) orbiting in electron clouds around the nucleus.

quizzes.q: The process of cell division is called:
quizzes.explanation: Mitosis is the process of cell division where one cell divides into two genetically identical daughter cells, each with the same number of chromosomes as the parent cell.

quizzes.q: What type of energy does a moving object have?
quizzes.explanation: A moving object has kinetic energy (energy of motion), given by KE = ½mv². The faster or heavier the object, the more kinetic energy it has.

quizzes.q: The periodic table organizes elements by:
quizzes.explanation: The periodic table organizes elements by increasing atomic number (number of protons). Elements in the same group have similar chemical properties.

quizzes.q: 'The rigid outer layer of Earth, including the crust and upper mantle. It consist...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Lithosphere' is defined as: The rigid outer layer of Earth, including the crust and upper mantle. It consists of tectonic plates that float on the semi-fluid asthenosphere below. Includes all rocks, minerals, and soil.

quizzes.q: 'All water on Earth — oceans (97.5% of all water), freshwater (lakes, rivers, str...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Hydrosphere' is defined as: All water on Earth — oceans (97.5% of all water), freshwater (lakes, rivers, streams), groundwater, glaciers, ice caps, and water vapor in the atmosphere. It is NOT just oceans.

quizzes.q: 'The layer of gases surrounding Earth. Composed of 78% nitrogen (N₂), 21% oxygen ...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Atmosphere' is defined as: The layer of gases surrounding Earth. Composed of 78% nitrogen (N₂), 21% oxygen (O₂), 0.9% argon (Ar), and 0.04% carbon dioxide (CO₂). Protects life from harmful UV radiation and regulates temperature.

quizzes.q: 'All living organisms on Earth and the environments in which they live. Includes ...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Biosphere' is defined as: All living organisms on Earth and the environments in which they live. Includes all ecosystems — from deep ocean vents to mountain peaks. It overlaps with all other spheres.

quizzes.q: 'Our solar system consists of the Sun, 8 planets, dwarf planets (like Pluto), ast...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Solar System' is defined as: Our solar system consists of the Sun, 8 planets, dwarf planets (like Pluto), asteroids, comets, and other objects held together by the Sun's gravity.

quizzes.q: 'A massive ball of hot gas (mainly hydrogen and helium) that produces energy thro...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Star' is defined as: A massive ball of hot gas (mainly hydrogen and helium) that produces energy through nuclear fusion — fusing hydrogen into helium in its core. Our Sun is a medium-sized star (yellow dwarf).

quizzes.q: 'A massive collection of stars, gas, dust, and dark matter held together by gravi...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Galaxy' is defined as: A massive collection of stars, gas, dust, and dark matter held together by gravity. Types: Spiral (like the Milky Way — barred spiral), Elliptical (older, less gas), and Irregular (no defined shape).

quizzes.q: 'The leading theory for the origin of the universe. About 13.8 billion years ago,...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Big Bang Theory' is defined as: The leading theory for the origin of the universe. About 13.8 billion years ago, all matter and energy was concentrated in a single point, which expanded rapidly. The universe continues to expand today.

quizzes.q: 'A symbiotic relationship where BOTH organisms benefit (+/+). Example: Clownfish ...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Mutualism' is defined as: A symbiotic relationship where BOTH organisms benefit (+/+). Example: Clownfish and sea anemone — the clownfish gets protection, the anemone gets food scraps and cleaning.

quizzes.q: 'A symbiotic relationship where one organism benefits and the other is neither he...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Commensalism' is defined as: A symbiotic relationship where one organism benefits and the other is neither helped nor harmed (+/0). Example: Barnacles on a whale — barnacles get transport to food-rich waters, the whale is unaffected.

quizzes.q: 'A symbiotic relationship where one organism (parasite) benefits and the other (h...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Parasitism' is defined as: A symbiotic relationship where one organism (parasite) benefits and the other (host) is harmed (+/−). Example: Tick on a dog — the tick feeds on blood, the dog loses blood and may get infected.

quizzes.q: 'When two organisms compete for the same resource (food, space, light, water). Bo...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Competition' is defined as: When two organisms compete for the same resource (food, space, light, water). Both are negatively affected (−/−). Example: Two plants growing close together compete for sunlight and nutrients.

quizzes.q: 'One organism (predator) hunts and eats another (prey). The predator benefits, th...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Predation' is defined as: One organism (predator) hunts and eats another (prey). The predator benefits, the prey is harmed. Example: Eagle catching a snake. This relationship drives natural selection.

quizzes.q: 'A species that has a disproportionately large effect on its ecosystem relative t...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Keystone Species' is defined as: A species that has a disproportionately large effect on its ecosystem relative to its abundance. Removing it causes dramatic changes. Example: Sea otters keep sea urchin populations in check, protecting kelp forests.

quizzes.q: 'A non-native species introduced to a new environment that causes harm to the eco...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Invasive Species' is defined as: A non-native species introduced to a new environment that causes harm to the ecosystem, economy, or human health. They often lack natural predators in the new environment.

quizzes.q: 'A graphical representation showing the amount of energy available at each trophi...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Energy Pyramid' is defined as: A graphical representation showing the amount of energy available at each trophic level. It is always upright because energy decreases at each level.

quizzes.q: 'Energy cannot be created or destroyed — only transformed from one form to anothe...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Conservation of Energy' is defined as: Energy cannot be created or destroyed — only transformed from one form to another. The total energy in a closed system remains constant. This is the First Law of Thermodynamics.

quizzes.q: 'Mass cannot be created or destroyed in a chemical reaction. Atoms are rearranged...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Conservation of Mass' is defined as: Mass cannot be created or destroyed in a chemical reaction. Atoms are rearranged but not created or destroyed. This is why equations must be balanced.

quizzes.explanation: This describes Star: A massive ball of hot gas (mainly hydrogen and helium) that produces energy through nuclear fusion — fusing hydrogen into helium in its core. Our Sun is a medium-sized star (yellow dwarf).

quizzes.q: Which concept is best illustrated by the following: 'A massive collection of stars, gas, dust, and dark matter he...'?
quizzes.explanation: This describes Galaxy: A massive collection of stars, gas, dust, and dark matter held together by gravity. Types: Spiral (like the Milky Way — barred spiral), Elliptical (older, less gas), and Irregular (no defined shape).

quizzes.q: Which concept is best illustrated by the following: 'The leading theory for the origin of the universe. About 13....'?
quizzes.explanation: This describes Big Bang Theory: The leading theory for the origin of the universe. About 13.8 billion years ago, all matter and energy was concentrated in a single point, which expanded rapidly. The universe continues to expand today.

quizzes.q: Which concept is best illustrated by the following: 'A symbiotic relationship where BOTH organisms benefit (+/+)....'?
quizzes.explanation: This describes Mutualism: A symbiotic relationship where BOTH organisms benefit (+/+). Example: Clownfish and sea anemone — the clownfish gets protection, the anemone gets food scraps and cleaning.

quizzes.q: Which concept is best illustrated by the following: 'A symbiotic relationship where one organism benefits and the...'?
quizzes.explanation: This describes Commensalism: A symbiotic relationship where one organism benefits and the other is neither helped nor harmed (+/0). Example: Barnacles on a whale — barnacles get transport to food-rich waters, the whale is unaffected.

quizzes.q: Which concept is best illustrated by the following: 'A symbiotic relationship where one organism (parasite) benef...'?
quizzes.explanation: This describes Parasitism: A symbiotic relationship where one organism (parasite) benefits and the other (host) is harmed (+/−). Example: Tick on a dog — the tick feeds on blood, the dog loses blood and may get infected.

quizzes.q: Which concept is best illustrated by the following: 'When two organisms compete for the same resource (food, spac...'?
quizzes.explanation: This describes Competition: When two organisms compete for the same resource (food, space, light, water). Both are negatively affected (−/−). Example: Two plants growing close together compete for sunlight and nutrients.

quizzes.q: Which concept is best illustrated by the following: 'One organism (predator) hunts and eats another (prey). The p...'?
quizzes.explanation: This describes Predation: One organism (predator) hunts and eats another (prey). The predator benefits, the prey is harmed. Example: Eagle catching a snake. This relationship drives natural selection.

quizzes.q: Which concept is best illustrated by the following: 'A species that has a disproportionately large effect on its ...'?
quizzes.explanation: This describes Keystone Species: A species that has a disproportionately large effect on its ecosystem relative to its abundance. Removing it causes dramatic changes. Example: Sea otters keep sea urchin populations in check, protecting kelp forests.

quizzes.q: Which concept is best illustrated by the following: 'A non-native species introduced to a new environment that ca...'?
quizzes.explanation: This describes Invasive Species: A non-native species introduced to a new environment that causes harm to the ecosystem, economy, or human health. They often lack natural predators in the new environment.

quizzes.q: Which concept is best illustrated by the following: 'A graphical representation showing the amount of energy avai...'?
quizzes.explanation: This describes Energy Pyramid: A graphical representation showing the amount of energy available at each trophic level. It is always upright because energy decreases at each level.

quizzes.q: Which concept is best illustrated by the following: 'Energy cannot be created or destroyed — only transformed fro...'?
quizzes.explanation: This describes Conservation of Energy: Energy cannot be created or destroyed — only transformed from one form to another. The total energy in a closed system remains constant. This is the First Law of Thermodynamics.

quizzes.q: Which concept is best illustrated by the following: 'Mass cannot be created or destroyed in a chemical reaction. ...'?
quizzes.explanation: This describes Conservation of Mass: Mass cannot be created or destroyed in a chemical reaction. Atoms are rearranged but not created or destroyed. This is why equations must be balanced.

chspecialized-45.name: Academic Strands: STEM, ABM, HUMSS, GAS

quizzes.q: STEM stands for:
quizzes.explanation: STEM is an acronym for Science, Technology, Engineering, and Mathematics. It represents a grouping of academic disciplines that are critical for innovation and competitiveness in the modern economy.

quizzes.q: ABM strand focuses on:
quizzes.explanation: ABM stands for Accountancy, Business, and Management. This strand prepares students for careers in business, finance, accounting, and entrepreneurship by teaching foundational concepts in these areas.

quizzes.q: HUMSS stands for:
quizzes.explanation: HUMSS stands for Humanities and Social Sciences. This academic track focuses on understanding human behavior, society, culture, and social issues through subjects like history, philosophy, political science, and sociology.

quizzes.q: How many parts does it have?
quizzes.explanation: The CET (College Entrance Test) typically has 3 parts covering different subject areas such as language proficiency, mathematics, and general knowledge. This structure allows for comprehensive assessment of student readiness.

quizzes.q: The GAS strand in SHS stands for:
quizzes.explanation: GAS stands for General Academic Strand. Unlike specialized tracks, GAS provides a well-rounded curriculum that draws from various academic disciplines, making it ideal for students exploring different career options.

quizzes.q: GAS stands for:
quizzes.explanation: GAS stands for General Academic Strand, one of the academic tracks in the Senior High School curriculum under the K-12 program in the Philippines. It offers a flexible curriculum combining subjects from different strands.

quizzes.q: Which career is most aligned with STEM?
quizzes.explanation: Engineering is most aligned with STEM because it directly applies principles of science, technology, engineering, and mathematics to solve real-world problems. While other careers may use some STEM skills, engineering is fundamentally built on these disciplines.

quizzes.q: ABM students typically study:
quizzes.explanation: ABM students study accounting, business, and economics subjects. These courses provide the foundation for understanding financial management, business operations, market economics, and entrepreneurial concepts.

quizzes.q: GAS strand is ideal for students who:
opts: Know exactly what career they want
quizzes.explanation: GAS is ideal for students who are undecided about their specialization. It provides exposure to various academic disciplines, helping students discover their interests before committing to a specific college course or career path.

quizzes.q: The Senior High School program in the Philippines has:
quizzes.explanation: Under the K-12 program implemented in the Philippines, Senior High School (SHS) consists of 2 years (Grades 11 and 12). This was added to the previous 10-year basic education system to align with international standards.

quizzes.q: Which is NOT a senior high school strand?
quizzes.explanation: MEDICAL is not an official senior high school strand. The recognized academic strands are STEM, ABM, HUMSS, and GAS. While there are science subjects in STEM, there is no dedicated 'Medical' strand at the SHS level.

quizzes.q: What subjects are emphasized in the STEM strand?
quizzes.explanation: STEM focuses on Science, Technology, Engineering, and Mathematics — including advanced math (calculus, trig), physics, chemistry, and biology.

quizzes.q: What career paths are common for STEM graduates?
quizzes.explanation: STEM opens doors to engineering, medicine, IT, research, architecture, and other science/tech-related professions.

quizzes.q: What is the focus of the ABM strand?
quizzes.explanation: ABM (Accountancy, Business, Management) prepares students for business-related courses — accounting, entrepreneurship, marketing, economics.

quizzes.q: What does HUMSS stand for and what does it focus on?
opts: Humanities and Social Sciences — literature, philosophy, history, political science
Home Economics and Management Skills Strand
Higher Understanding of Math and Science Studies
quizzes.explanation: HUMSS (Humanities and Social Sciences) focuses on social sciences, literature, philosophy, history, communication — preparing for law, education, journalism.

quizzes.q: What is the GAS strand designed for?
opts: Students who are undecided — provides a general curriculum across disciplines
Students who want to study gas engineering
quizzes.explanation: GAS (General Academic Strand) is for students still exploring their interests. It offers a mix of subjects from different tracks.

quizzes.q: Which strand should a student choose if they want to become a Certified Public Accountant (CPA)?
quizzes.explanation: ABM is the natural pathway for aspiring CPAs — it covers accounting principles, business math, economics, and organizational management.

quizzes.q: Which strand is best for a student interested in pursuing Law in college?
quizzes.explanation: HUMSS is the recommended pre-law strand — it covers political science, philosophy, logic, ethics, and communication skills needed for law school.

quizzes.q: What CET-relevant subjects does the STEM strand cover?
opts: Calculus, Trig, Statistics for Math; Physics, Chemistry, Bio for Science
quizzes.explanation: STEM CET focus: Math = Calculus, Trigonometry, Statistics. Science = Physics, Chemistry, Biology (advanced level). These align closely with CET content.

chspecialized-46.name: College Programs: Engineering, Business, Arts, Health & Education

quizzes.q: An engineering program typically requires strong skills in:
quizzes.explanation: Engineering programs demand strong mathematics and science skills because engineers apply mathematical principles and scientific knowledge to design, build, and maintain structures, machines, and systems. Calculus, physics, and chemistry are fundamental requirements.

quizzes.q: A BSN program prepares students to become:
quizzes.explanation: BSN stands for Bachelor of Science in Nursing. This program prepares students to become registered nurses by teaching patient care, medical procedures, health assessment, and nursing theory, along with clinical practice.

quizzes.q: TVL stands for:
quizzes.explanation: TVL stands for Technical-Vocational-Livelihood. It is one of the tracks in Senior High School that provides students with practical, job-ready skills in various technical and vocational fields for immediate employment or entrepreneurship.

quizzes.q: Which is a TVL specialization?
quizzes.explanation: Home Economics is one of the specializations under the TVL track. It covers areas like cookery, food and beverage services, tourism, dressmaking, and other livelihood skills. Other TVL specializations include ICT, Industrial Arts, and Agri-Fishery.

quizzes.q: The advantage of TVL is:
quizzes.explanation: The main advantage of TVL is that it provides hands-on, practical skills for employment. Students gain real-world experience and industry-relevant competencies that make them job-ready upon graduation, and they can earn TESDA certifications.

quizzes.q: Which is NOT a TVL track?
quizzes.explanation: Pre-Medicine is not a TVL track. The recognized TVL specializations are Home Economics, ICT, Industrial Arts, and Agri-Fishery Arts. Pre-Medicine is more aligned with the STEM academic track at the college level.

quizzes.q: 'The application of math, science, and technology to design, build, and maintain ...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Engineering' is defined as: The application of math, science, and technology to design, build, and maintain structures, machines, systems, and processes that solve real-world problems.

quizzes.q: 'Designs and builds infrastructure — roads, bridges, buildings, dams. Board exam:...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Civil Engineering (CE)' is defined as: Designs and builds infrastructure — roads, bridges, buildings, dams. Board exam: CE Licensure Exam (CELE). Career: structural engineer, construction manager, geotechnical engineer. High demand due to ongoing infrastructure projects (Build Build Build program).

quizzes.q: 'Deals with electrical systems, power generation, transmission, and distribution....' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Electrical Engineering (EE)' is defined as: Deals with electrical systems, power generation, transmission, and distribution. Board exam: EE Licensure Exam. Career: power plant engineer, distribution engineer, control systems engineer. Critical for the energy sector.

quizzes.q: 'Covers machines, HVAC systems, manufacturing, and robotics. Board exam: ME Licen...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Mechanical Engineering (ME)' is defined as: Covers machines, HVAC systems, manufacturing, and robotics. Board exam: ME Licensure Exam. Career: manufacturing engineer, HVAC designer, automotive engineer. Versatile — applies to nearly every industry.

quizzes.q: 'Applies chemistry and physics to process raw materials into useful products — fu...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Chemical Engineering (ChemE)' is defined as: Applies chemistry and physics to process raw materials into useful products — fuels, food, pharmaceuticals. Board exam: ChemE Licensure Exam. Career: process engineer, quality control, refinery engineer.

quizzes.q: 'Bridges hardware and software — computer architecture, embedded systems, network...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Computer Engineering (CpE)' is defined as: Bridges hardware and software — computer architecture, embedded systems, networking. Board exam: CpE Licensure Exam (newer). Career: hardware engineer, firmware developer, systems architect. Overlaps with CS and IT.

quizzes.q: 'Focuses on algorithms, data structures, AI, machine learning, and software devel...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Computer Science (CS)' is defined as: Focuses on algorithms, data structures, AI, machine learning, and software development. No board exam (industry certifications optional). Career: software developer, data scientist, AI engineer. Highest starting salary among programs.

quizzes.q: 'Practical application of technology — web development, database management, netw...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Information Technology (IT)' is defined as: Practical application of technology — web development, database management, networking, cybersecurity. No board exam. Career: IT specialist, web developer, systems administrator, network engineer.

quizzes.q: 'The study of financial recording, auditing, taxation, and reporting. Graduates t...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Accountancy (BSA)' is defined as: The study of financial recording, auditing, taxation, and reporting. Graduates take the Certified Public Accountant (CPA) Licensure Exam — one of the hardest board exams in the Philippines with a national passing rate of 25–40%.

quizzes.q: 'Covers management, marketing, finance, operations, and human resources. No board...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Business Administration (BSBA)' is defined as: Covers management, marketing, finance, operations, and human resources. No board exam but offers specializations: Marketing Management, Financial Management, Operations Management, HR Management.

quizzes.q: 'The study of starting and managing businesses — business planning, venture capit...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Entrepreneurship' is defined as: The study of starting and managing businesses — business planning, venture capital, innovation, and small business management. No board exam. Focuses on practical business creation skills.

quizzes.q: 'A 4-year degree focused on traditional and contemporary art — painting, sculptur...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Fine Arts (BFA)' is defined as: A 4-year degree focused on traditional and contemporary art — painting, sculpture, printmaking, photography, and mixed media. Includes art history and criticism. No board exam but portfolio-based admission.

quizzes.q: 'A 5-year degree program that combines art and engineering to design buildings an...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Architecture (BS Architecture)' is defined as: A 5-year degree program that combines art and engineering to design buildings and structures. Graduates take the Architecture Licensure Exam (ALE). Passing rate: 50–65%. Career: architect, urban planner, interior architect.

quizzes.q: 'Covers digital design, animation, video production, web design, and interactive ...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Multimedia Arts' is defined as: Covers digital design, animation, video production, web design, and interactive media. No board exam. Career: graphic designer, animator, video editor, UX/UI designer. High demand in advertising, gaming, and BPO.

quizzes.q: 'Designs consumer products — furniture, appliances, electronics, packaging. Focus...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Industrial Design' is defined as: Designs consumer products — furniture, appliances, electronics, packaging. Focuses on form, function, usability, and aesthetics. No board exam. Career: product designer, furniture designer, packaging designer.

quizzes.q: 'A 4-year program preparing students to provide patient care in hospitals, clinic...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Nursing (BSN)' is defined as: A 4-year program preparing students to provide patient care in hospitals, clinics, and communities. Board exam: Nurse Licensure Exam (NLE). Passing rate: 70–85%. Career: staff nurse, ward nurse, community health nurse. High overseas demand (US, UK, Middle East, Japan).

quizzes.q: 'Studies drug formulation, dispensing, pharmacology, and pharmaceutical care. Boa...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Pharmacy (BS Pharmacy)' is defined as: Studies drug formulation, dispensing, pharmacology, and pharmaceutical care. Board exam: Pharmacist Licensure Exam. Passing rate: 50–70%. Career: hospital pharmacist, community pharmacist, pharmaceutical industry, drug inspector.

quizzes.q: 'Laboratory science — analyzing blood, tissue, and body fluids for disease diagno...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Medical Technology (BS MedTech)' is defined as: Laboratory science — analyzing blood, tissue, and body fluids for disease diagnosis. Board exam: MedTech Licensure Exam. Passing rate: 60–80%. Career: medical technologist, clinical laboratory scientist, quality assurance. High demand in hospitals and diagnostic labs.

quizzes.q: 'Rehabilitation science — restoring movement and function after injury or illness...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Physical Therapy (BSPT)' is defined as: Rehabilitation science — restoring movement and function after injury or illness. Board exam: PT Licensure Exam. Passing rate: 55–75%. Career: physical therapist, rehabilitation specialist, sports therapist. Growing demand due to aging population and sports medicine.

quizzes.q: 'Medical imaging — X-ray, CT scan, MRI, ultrasound. Board exam: RadTech Licensure...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Radiologic Technology (BS RadTech)' is defined as: Medical imaging — X-ray, CT scan, MRI, ultrasound. Board exam: RadTech Licensure Exam. Passing rate: 60–75%. Career: radiologic technologist, CT/MRI technologist, sonographer.

quizzes.q: 'Prepares teachers for elementary school (Grades 1–6). Covers child development, ...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'BEEd (Bachelor of Elementary Education)' is defined as: Prepares teachers for elementary school (Grades 1–6). Covers child development, teaching methods, curriculum design, and classroom management. Specializations include General Education, Special Education (SPED), and Pre-school Education. Board exam: LET.

quizzes.q: 'Prepares teachers for junior and senior high school (Grades 7–12). Students majo...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'BSEd (Bachelor of Secondary Education)' is defined as: Prepares teachers for junior and senior high school (Grades 7–12). Students major in a specific subject: English, Math, Science, Filipino, Social Studies, or Values Education. Board exam: LET.

quizzes.q: 'The education-specific subjects in teacher preparation — foundations of educatio...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'Professional Education (Prof Ed)' is defined as: The education-specific subjects in teacher preparation — foundations of education, principles of teaching, child/adolescent development, educational technology, curriculum development, assessment, and classroom management.

quizzes.q: Which is NOT a TVL track?
quizzes.explanation: ABM (Accountancy, Business, and Management) is not a TVL track—it is a separate academic strand. The TVL tracks include Home Economics (HE), ICT, Industrial Arts, and Agri-Fishery. ABM focuses on business education rather than technical-vocational skills.

quizzes.q: TVET stands for:
quizzes.explanation: TVET stands for Technical-Vocational Education and Training. It is a comprehensive term used internationally to describe education and training programs that equip learners with practical skills and knowledge for specific occupations or trades.

quizzes.q: Which is NOT a visual art form?
quizzes.explanation: Accounting is not a visual art form. It is a business and financial discipline. Painting, sculpture, and photography are all recognized visual art forms that involve creative expression through visual media.

quizzes.q: The color wheel was developed by:
quizzes.explanation: Sir Isaac Newton developed the first color wheel in 1666. He created it by splitting white light through a prism to reveal the spectrum of colors, then arranged them in a circular format. This became foundational for color theory in art and science.

quizzes.q: Primary colors are:
quizzes.explanation: In traditional color theory, the primary colors are red, blue, and yellow. These colors cannot be created by mixing other colors and serve as the foundation for creating all other colors through mixing. (In light/RGB, they are red, green, and blue.)

quizzes.q: Secondary colors are made by:
quizzes.explanation: Secondary colors are created by mixing two primary colors together. For example: red + blue = purple, red + yellow = orange, and blue + yellow = green. This principle is fundamental to color theory in art.

quizzes.q: Complementary colors are:
opts: Next to each other on the wheel
quizzes.explanation: Complementary colors are positioned opposite each other on the color wheel (e.g., red and green, blue and orange, yellow and purple). When placed side by side, they create maximum contrast and visual vibrancy. When mixed, they neutralize each other.

quizzes.q: 'A Senior High School strand focused on physical education, sports science, coach...' — What term is being described?

quizzes.q: 'Study of exercise physiology, sports psychology, biomechanics, and movement scie...' — What term is being described?

quizzes.q: 'The scientific study of human performance — nutrition for athletes, hydration, i...' — What term is being described?

quizzes.q: 'Teaching sports techniques, developing game strategies, motivating athletes, and...' — What term is being described?

quizzes.q: 'The business side of sports — event organization, facility management, sports ma...' — What term is being described?

quizzes.q: 'Technical Education and Skills Development Authority — the Philippine government...' — What term is being described?

quizzes.q: 'Government agency for sports development. Funds national athletes, builds sports...' — What term is being described?
quizzes.explanation: 'PSC (Philippine Sports Commission)' is defined as: Government agency for sports development. Funds national athletes, builds sports facilities, and implements grassroots sports programs. Created by RA 6847 (1990).

quizzes.q: 'The National Olympic Committee of the Philippines. Responsible for sending athle...' — What term is being described?

quizzes.q: 'Traditional and contemporary art forms — drawing, painting, sculpture, printmaki...' — What term is being described?

quizzes.q: 'Digital and electronic creative works — graphic design, animation, film/video pr...' — What term is being described?

quizzes.q: 'Written creative expression — fiction, poetry, creative non-fiction, playwriting...' — What term is being described?

quizzes.q: 'Live performance disciplines — theater, dance, music (vocal and instrumental), a...' — What term is being described?

quizzes.q: 'Pros: Immediate income, real-world experience, no tuition costs. Cons: Lower sta...' — What term is being described?

quizzes.q: 'Pros: Higher lifetime earnings, professional licensure, career advancement. Cons...' — What term is being described?
